title: The Mentor's Handbook
subtitle: Teaching Kernel Engineering — everything a mentor must learn, from matrix multiplication up, to deliver the workshop.

parts.title: Before You Teach
parts.blurb: The mentor's mindset, how to use this handbook, and the single story that connects all fourteen sessions so you always know where you are.

chapters.title: How to use this handbook
chapters.blurb: The seven ingredients in every chapter, how to read it (learn first, then teach), and how to prep a session from it.

chapters.title: The mentor's mindset: teaching hard things simply
chapters.blurb: You only truly understand something when you can teach it to a beginner. How to build from zero, use metaphors, and turn abstraction into arithmetic on a whiteboard.

chapters.title: The whole arc: one story, fourteen sessions
chapters.blurb: The single narrative — 'make the matmul fast, then make the model fast' — that ties every lecture and workshop together, drawn as one map.

parts.title: Foundations You Must Feel
parts.blurb: The bedrock. Matrix multiplication from the absolute beginning, why it is the whole game, what 'fast' really means, and the compute-vs-memory idea that governs everything. Feel these in your bones before anything else.

chapters.title: Matrix multiplication, from the absolute beginning
chapters.blurb: Dot products, the shape dance, a 2×2 by hand, then why C = A·B is three nested loops. The one operation everything else optimizes.

chapters.title: Why matrix multiply is the whole game
chapters.blurb: Every linear layer, attention score, and MLP is a matmul. Trace a transformer and count the matmuls. This is why kernels matter.

chapters.title: What 'fast' actually means: time, FLOPs, bytes
chapters.blurb: Operations, floating-point ops per second, bytes moved, and why a stopwatch is not enough. The units a mentor must own.

chapters.title: Compute vs memory: the kitchen that governs everything
chapters.blurb: The three regimes (compute / memory / overhead) told as a kitchen story — cooking vs fetching ingredients. The master mental model.

chapters.title: The numbers to know cold
chapters.blurb: 989 TFLOP/s, 3.35 TB/s, 132 SMs, 228 KiB, 32 threads. The handful of H100 numbers that turn hand-waving into intuition.

chapters.title: The roofline, drawn simply
chapters.blurb: The one plot that says the fastest your kernel could ever go — a ceiling of compute and a ramp of memory — explained without intimidation.

parts.title: The Machine
parts.blurb: What a GPU actually is, taught with pictures. Threads and warps, the memory hierarchy, tensor cores, and latency hiding — the vocabulary and intuition students need before any code.

chapters.title: CPU vs GPU: the chef and the cafeteria
chapters.blurb: A few brilliant chefs versus a thousand line cooks. Why GPUs trade cleverness for overwhelming parallelism, and when that wins.

chapters.title: Threads, warps & blocks: the marching band
chapters.blurb: One thread = one musician; a warp of 32 marches in lockstep; blocks share a room. The execution hierarchy as a parade.

chapters.title: The memory hierarchy: your desk, drawers, shelves, warehouse
chapters.blurb: Registers, shared memory, L2, HBM as distances you walk for a tool. Why the whole craft is keeping data close.

chapters.title: Tensor cores: the matmul machine inside the machine
chapters.blurb: A special unit that eats little matrices whole. Where 95% of the FLOPs live and why modern kernels are built around it.

chapters.title: Latency hiding: the short-order cook juggling tickets
chapters.blurb: Memory is slow, so the GPU keeps dozens of jobs in flight and always works on a ready one. Why 'occupancy' matters.

chapters.title: Coalescing: everybody boards the same bus
chapters.blurb: When 32 threads read neighbouring addresses, the hardware fetches them in one trip. Scattered reads waste the bus. The cheapest big win.

parts.title: Teaching the GEMM Ladder
parts.blurb: The heart of the course, session by session. For each rung of the ladder: the idea in plain words, the metaphor, the board work, the live demo, the number, and where students trip.

chapters.title: Teaching Kernel 1: the honest, slow start
chapters.blurb: One thread per output. How to run it, show it hitting 1.3% of cuBLAS, and make students hungry to fix it.

chapters.title: Teaching Kernel 2: the one-line miracle
chapters.blurb: Remap two indices, 6× faster. How to build the suspense and the reveal, and draw the coalesced vs scattered bus.

chapters.title: Teaching Kernel 3: the shared whiteboard
chapters.blurb: Stage tiles in shared memory so the block reuses them. The reuse idea, drawn, and why we block over K.

chapters.title: Teaching Kernels 4–5: pockets full of work
chapters.blurb: Each thread computes many outputs from values held in registers. 1D then 2D tiling, and the arithmetic-intensity climb to 68.7%.

chapters.title: Teaching Kernel 6: carry four boxes at once
chapters.blurb: float4 loads and the SASS 'eight loads become two' moment — the most satisfying reveal in the course.

chapters.title: Teaching Kernels 7–8: the org chart
chapters.blurb: Autotuning and warptiling — making block, warp, and thread explicit to reach 94% of cuBLAS. How to land the finale.

chapters.title: Teaching the tensor-core rebuild
chapters.blurb: Do the ladder again on tensor cores: fragments, swizzling, mma.sync. How to teach WMMA without drowning students.

parts.title: Teaching Inference Kernels
parts.blurb: Where the GEMM skills meet real LLMs. Fusion, softmax, attention and FlashAttention, and why chatting with a model is a memory problem — all in teachable pictures.

chapters.title: Teaching fusion: stop driving to the warehouse
chapters.blurb: Why round-tripping tensors through HBM between tiny ops is the waste that fusion removes. The single biggest inference win.

chapters.title: Teaching softmax & the online trick
chapters.blurb: Stable softmax by hand, then the running-max/running-sum trick that makes FlashAttention possible. Teach it as a running average.

chapters.title: Teaching attention as three matmuls
chapters.blurb: QKᵀ → softmax → V, and why the giant N×N scores are the problem. Set up FlashAttention as the fix.

chapters.title: Teaching FlashAttention: never write the scores down
chapters.blurb: Streaming attention in tiles so the N×N matrix never touches memory. The metaphor, the tiling, and the production stakes.

chapters.title: Teaching the KV cache: why chat is memory-bound
chapters.blurb: Prefill vs decode, the KV cache, and why generating each token re-reads a growing memory. PagedAttention as the fix.

parts.title: Teaching the Frontier
parts.blurb: So you sound current and credible. Hopper and Blackwell in plain words, DeepSeek's real kernels, AI that writes kernels, and how to debug — each tied to what shipped this year.

chapters.title: Teaching Hopper: the async delivery truck (TMA & WGMMA)
chapters.blurb: How the H100 added a hardware copy engine and warpgroup matmul, in words a student can hold. Why FlashAttention-3 needs it.

chapters.title: Teaching Blackwell & NVFP4: four-bit numbers that work
chapters.blurb: Tensor memory, and micro-scaled 4-bit formats. The 2000µs→22µs hackathon story as your hook.

chapters.title: Teaching DeepSeek & DSpark: kernels behind a frontier model
chapters.blurb: FlashMLA, DeepGEMM, and DSpark = speculative decoding. How an open lab ships its own kernels — the ultimate production link.

chapters.title: Teaching AI that writes kernels
chapters.blurb: KernelBench, the 'monkeys' that sample 100 kernels, and the honest wins-and-failures. The human+AI+profiler loop — and the tie to harnesses.

chapters.title: Teaching debugging: war stories that stick
chapters.blurb: The race, the misaligned load, the silent NaN. compute-sanitizer, core dumps and cuda-gdb — taught as detective stories.

parts.title: Delivery Craft
parts.blurb: Turning understanding into a great live session. Minute-by-minute plans for all eight lectures and six workshops, the questions students always ask, and how to run the capstone.

chapters.title: Lecture plans: L1–L4, minute by minute
chapters.blurb: Timings, board sequence, the one demo, and the checkpoint questions for the first four lectures.

chapters.title: Lecture plans: L5–L8, minute by minute
chapters.blurb: The GEMM finale, tensor cores, profiling/debugging, and attention — paced for a three-hour room.

chapters.title: Workshop plans: the six deep-dives
chapters.blurb: FlashAttention, Hopper, the abstraction ladder, serving kernels, Blackwell/NVFP4, and the DeepSeek+AI finale — how to run each hands-on.

chapters.title: The questions students always ask (with crisp answers)
chapters.blurb: A ready bank of the recurring questions and the tight, correct answers so you're never caught flat.

chapters.title: Running the 'You vs the machine' capstone
chapters.blurb: How to set up, mentor, and grade the by-hand-plus-LLM kernel capstone on the worklog, not the raw speedup.
